title: Portfolio & Resume Guidance
eli8:
# 📄 Show the World What You Can Build!

Imagine you're a carpenter and someone asks "can you make furniture?" You COULD explain it in words... or you could just show them the beautiful bookshelf you already built! A portfolio is YOUR bookshelf — proof that you can build with AI!

## 💻 The Best Portfolio Projects

Here are the projects that make employers go "wow!":

**Project 1: RAG Chatbot** 🤖
- Pick any topic you love (anime, sports stats, cooking recipes)
- Build a chatbot that answers questions about YOUR documents
- Show that it cites its sources and doesn't make stuff up!

**Project 2: Fine-tuned Model** 🎨
- Take a base model and fine-tune it on something cool
- Show before/after examples: "Here's what the base model said... here's what MY model says"

**Project 3: AI Agent** 🕵️
- Build an agent that does a real task (research assistant, code helper, data analyst)
- Show it actually solving a problem step by step!

## 📸 Make GitHub Look Great

Employers WILL look at your GitHub profile:
- 📌 **Pin your best 6 projects** to the top
- 📖 Every project needs a **README** that explains:
  - What problem does it solve?
  - How does it work? (with a diagram!)
  - How do I run it?
- 🎬 A **demo GIF** is worth a thousand words!

## 📝 Your AI Resume Keywords

Make sure these words are in your resume (if you've done them):
`RAG` `LangChain` `FastAPI` `RAGAS` `fine-tuning` `LoRA` `embeddings` `vector database` `prompt engineering` `LLM evaluation`

And add **numbers**! "Reduced hallucination rate by 35%" is 10× better than "improved the AI"! 📊
detail:
## Building Your LLM Engineering Portfolio and Resume

### Portfolio Projects That Stand Out

**1. RAG Chatbot over Custom Documents**
- *What it shows*: end-to-end LLM engineering, retrieval, prompt engineering, evaluation
- *Implementation*: ingest custom corpus → Chroma/Pinecone → FastAPI → evaluation with RAGAS
- *Differentiation*: choose an unusual domain (legal contracts, academic papers, game wikis)
- *Evaluation metrics to show*: faithfulness score, answer relevance, latency benchmarks

**2. Fine-tuned Model with Before/After Comparison**
- *What it shows*: understanding of when fine-tuning is appropriate, data preparation, evaluation
- *Implementation*: collect 500-2000 training examples → LoRA fine-tuning on Llama 3 → MMLU or domain benchmark
- *Differentiation*: quantify improvement with clear metrics, show failure modes of the base model

**3. AI Agent That Automates a Real Task**
- *What it shows*: agent design, tool integration, prompt engineering, reliability engineering
- *Examples*: research assistant (searches web + summarizes), data analysis agent (reads CSV + creates visualizations), code generation + test runner
- *Key element*: show the agent handling failures gracefully, not just the happy path

**4. LLM Evaluation Framework**
- *What it shows*: rigorous thinking about quality, production mindset
- *Implementation*: RAGAS evaluation pipeline over a benchmark dataset, CI integration
- *Differentiation*: less common project = stands out

### Project README Template

Every project README should include:
```markdown
# Project Name

## Problem
[1-2 sentences: what problem does this solve?]

## Architecture
[Diagram or text description of components]

## Tech Stack
- LLM: Claude Haiku / GPT-4o mini
- Embeddings: text-embedding-3-small
- Vector DB: Chroma (local) / Pinecone (production)
- API: FastAPI
- Evaluation: RAGAS

## Results
- Faithfulness: 0.87
- Answer Relevance: 0.82
- P95 Latency: 1.2s

## Demo
[GIF or link]

## Run Locally
[3-5 commands to get it running]
```

### GitHub Profile Optimization

- Pin 4-6 best repositories (use the ⭐ pin feature)
- Profile README with your skills and current focus
- Consistent commit history (even small improvements count)
- Demo GIFs in README — use `asciinema` for terminal or `LICEcap` for desktop
- Semantic commit messages: `feat(rag): add cross-encoder reranking`

### Resume for LLM Engineer Roles

**Essential keywords** (use if you have experience):
`RAG` `LangChain` `LlamaIndex` `FastAPI` `RAGAS` `LangSmith` `vector embeddings` `fine-tuning` `LoRA` `PEFT` `prompt engineering` `LLM evaluation` `sentence-transformers` `Chroma` `Pinecone` `vLLM`

**Quantify everything**:
- "Reduced hallucination rate by 35% using RAGAS-based evaluation pipeline"
- "Built RAG system over 50K documents with P95 latency under 1.5s"
- "Fine-tuned Llama 3 on 1,200 domain examples, achieving 23% improvement on domain benchmark"
- "Reduced LLM API costs by 60% through model routing and prompt caching"

**Job titles to search for**:
- LLM Engineer / Large Language Model Engineer
- AI Engineer
- Generative AI Engineer
- ML Engineer (LLM focus)
- Applied AI Engineer
- Prompt Engineer (entry level)
- AI Product Engineer

### Where to Keep Learning

**Free courses**:
- **fast.ai** — Practical Deep Learning for Coders (bottom-up, code-first)
- **Hugging Face courses** — NLP, Diffusion, Agents (free, hands-on)
- **DeepLearning.AI short courses** — LangChain, RAG, Agents, Evals (1-2 hours each)
- **Anthropic courses** — Prompt engineering, tool use (official, free)

**Stay current**:
- Follow @karpathy, @swyx, @simonw, @LangChainAI on Twitter/X
- Hugging Face Daily Papers (papers.huggingface.co)
- AI newsletters: Import AI, The Batch, TLDR AI
- arXiv cs.CL, cs.AI sections

**Community**:
- Hugging Face Discord — active, welcoming
- Local AI/ML meetups (meetup.com)
- Build in public on Twitter/LinkedIn — share what you're learning
- Contribute to open-source (LangChain, LlamaIndex, RAGAS) — huge resume signal

### The Portfolio Flywheel

```
Build project → Document it well → Share publicly
     ↑                                     |
     |                                     ↓
Learning from         ←       Network/feedback/
feedback/issues               visibility from
                              GitHub + social
```

Building in public compounds: each project you share builds your reputation, attracts collaborators, and creates interview talking points.

mcqs.question: Which portfolio project most effectively demonstrates end-to-end LLM engineering skills to a potential employer?
options: A Jupyter notebook showing exploration of the OpenAI API
A RAG chatbot with custom documents, RAGAS evaluation metrics, a FastAPI backend, and a clear README with architecture and benchmarks
A blog post summarizing recent LLM papers
A collection of creative prompt templates saved as text files
mcqs.explanation: A complete RAG system with evaluation demonstrates: document processing, retrieval, prompting, API design, and production thinking. It shows the full stack. A Jupyter notebook or blog post doesn't demonstrate engineering skills as concretely.

mcqs.question: When writing a resume bullet for an LLM project, which version is strongest?
options: "Worked on an AI chatbot using various LLM technologies"
"Built a chatbot that helps users find information"
"Developed RAG pipeline over 50K support documents achieving faithfulness score 0.88, reducing escalation rate by 28%"
"Familiar with LangChain, FastAPI, and vector databases"
mcqs.explanation: Quantified, specific results are the strongest signal. The third option specifies the technical approach (RAG), scale (50K documents), quality metric (faithfulness 0.88), and business impact (28% reduced escalation). The others are vague and unmeasurable.

mcqs.question: What makes a fine-tuning project stand out in a portfolio compared to just saying 'I fine-tuned Llama 3'?
options: Using a larger model — 70B parameters demonstrates more skill than 7B
A before/after comparison with concrete evaluation metrics showing what the base model failed at and how fine-tuning improved it
Fine-tuning on a larger dataset — number of examples is the key signal
Using the most recent fine-tuning technique (RLHF is always more impressive than LoRA)
mcqs.explanation: What employers want to see is: (1) you identified a problem, (2) gathered quality data, (3) applied fine-tuning, (4) measured the improvement rigorously. The before/after comparison with metrics tells that complete story.

mcqs.question: Which learning resource is best described as 'official prompt engineering guidance from the model provider'?
options: fast.ai Practical Deep Learning
DeepLearning.AI LangChain course
Anthropic's prompt engineering documentation and courses
Hugging Face NLP course
mcqs.explanation: Anthropic publishes official prompt engineering guides, a Prompt Library, and short courses covering best practices for Claude — directly from the model creator. This is the most authoritative source for prompting Claude specifically.

mcqs.question: Why is contributing to open-source projects like LangChain or RAGAS a strong resume signal for LLM engineer roles?
options: Because it proves the candidate has a high GitHub follower count
Open-source contributions demonstrate real engineering judgment, ability to work in a codebase with standards, and public code reviewers have validated the work
Because all LLM engineering roles require open-source experience as a hiring prerequisite
It shows the candidate prefers working for free
mcqs.explanation: Open-source contributions show: you can navigate a real codebase, your code was reviewed and merged by senior engineers (a quality signal), you understand the tools deeply enough to improve them, and you have a concrete, verifiable work artifact.

mcqs.question: What should every project README include to make it most effective for a portfolio?
options: Only the license information and installation instructions
Problem statement, architecture description, tech stack, result metrics, demo GIF, and minimal run instructions
A detailed academic literature review of related work
The complete source code repeated in the README for convenience
mcqs.explanation: A strong README answers: What problem? How does it work? (architecture) What did you build it with? How well does it work? (metrics) What does it look like? (demo) How do I run it? Employers spend 30-60 seconds on a project — make every element count.

flashcards.back: A collection of completed, publicly accessible projects that demonstrate applied skills. For LLM engineers: GitHub repositories with working code, evaluation metrics, architecture documentation, and live demos where possible.
Include: problem statement, architecture diagram, tech stack, result metrics, demo GIF, and 3-5 commands to run locally. Pin 4-6 best repos to your profile. Consistent commit history matters.

RAG, LangChain, LlamaIndex, FastAPI, RAGAS, LangSmith, fine-tuning, LoRA, PEFT, prompt engineering, vector embeddings, sentence-transformers, Chroma, Pinecone, vLLM. Use only if genuinely experienced.
The most common and impressive LLM portfolio project: custom document corpus → ingestion pipeline → vector database → query API → RAGAS evaluation. Demonstrates the full engineering stack.

Resume bullets with numbers are far stronger than vague claims. Example: 'Reduced hallucination rate by 35% using RAGAS-based evaluation pipeline' vs. 'Improved AI quality.' Always include scale, metric, and result.
A platform offering 1-2 hour free courses on applied LLM topics: RAG, LangChain, LLM Evals, Agents, Prompt Engineering — taught by authors of the major frameworks. Excellent for staying current.

Twitter/X AI community (follow Karpathy, swyx, Simon Willison), Hugging Face Discord, local AI meetups, building in public (sharing projects on social). Network compounds — early visibility creates opportunities.

LLM Engineer, AI Engineer, Generative AI Engineer, ML Engineer (LLM focus), Applied AI Engineer, Prompt Engineer (entry), AI Product Engineer. Search all variants — companies use different naming conventions.

videos.title: AI Career Path and LLM Engineering Skills
videos.description: Karpathy's overview of the LLM landscape and the skills practitioners need — useful framing for what to prioritize learning and building as you develop your AI engineering career.

videos.title: Building an AI Portfolio That Gets You Hired
videos.description: Practical guidance on structuring portfolio projects, writing impactful GitHub READMEs, optimizing your resume for AI roles, and what hiring managers actually look for in candidates.
